text: This video will demonstrate the laparoscopic approach for a closed-loop small bowel obstruction. A seventy-nine-year-old female presented to the hospital with two days of abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She had tenderness to palpation in the lower abdomen. Her surgical history included a hysterectomy that had been performed many years in the past. A CT scan was performed which revealed a distended loop of intestine concerning for a closed-loop obstruction.
During laparoscopic exploration, the surgeon observes ischemic bowel in the pelvis, characterized by necrotic tissue and hemorrhagic mesentery.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to transect the adhesion from the patient's prior hysterectomy, unraveling it to relieve the closed-loop obstruction. The other end of the adhesion band is transected, allowing the bowel to be delivered from the pelvis.

The surgeon visualizes the extent of bowel injury after resolving the obstruction, highlighting one of the two transition points. Significant bowel ischemia and mesenteric necrosis are observed, prompting the decision to plan a resection.

A 12mm laparoscopic port is placed to accommodate the stapler. The surgeon begins mesenteric transection, creating a window at a level of healthy intestine. The mesentery is completely transected, and the laparoscopic stapler is positioned across the intestine for resection.

The powered stapler is used to make precise adjustments within the abdomen, facilitating the transection of the bowel at the specified level. The surgeon continues the mesenteric dissection, transecting the mesentery below the necrotic area to ensure a proper seal and prevent bleeding.

The surgeon continues dissection until reaching an area of healthy intestine. The laparoscopic stapler is introduced into the abdomen to transect the bowel. Final mesenteric attachments are ligated using an energy device, and the specimen is moved away from the operative field.

An assistant helps create a window on the anti-mesenteric border of the small intestine. A small piece of bowel is removed from the abdomen under direct visualization. Another window is made at the opposite staple line, and the bowel is aligned in an anti-peristaltic fashion to prepare for intracorporeal anastomosis.

The surgeon, assisted by an assistant, uses two graspers to carefully position the stapler through the intestine, ensuring the bowel remains stable. Once properly aligned, the common channel is formed.

The surgeon reintroduces the stapler into the intestine to lengthen the common channel to approximately ten centimeters. The stapler is then removed, and the anastomosis is visualized, showing no signs of bleeding.

The surgeon grasps the bowel and uses a stapler to close the common enterotomy. The stapler is fired, closing approximately seventy-five percent of the enterotomy. Both sides of the bowel are examined to confirm proper closure.
The surgeon reintroduces the stapler to complete the anastomosis, utilizing the initially long common channel to ensure the integrity of the connection between the bowel segments.
The completed small bowel anastomosis is visible, showing a widely patent connection. The surgeon grasps both the small portion of bowel from the anastomosis closure and the main specimen.

The surgeon enlarges the twelve millimeter port site and places a small wound protector. A Babcock clamp is used through the wound protector to extract the specimens without creating a larger extraction site. Following specimen removal, the patient's pelvis is thoroughly irrigated and suctioned.

The surgeon examines the bowel, confirming the anastomosis is in excellent position without any twisting of the mesentery. The widely patent anastomosis is visible. The two leaves of the small mesenteric defect are grasped and closed using a twelve millimeter clip applier.
The surgeon places clips to close the mesenteric defect completely, with the final closure of the defect visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon removes the wound protector from the patient's abdomen through the twelve millimeter port site. The large fascial defect is visible, representing a potential site for future herniation. An endoclosed device with absorbable suture is used to close the defect in a figure of eight pattern.

The video displays the patient's postoperative abdomen with a red circle highlighting the left upper quadrant incision, identified as the extraction site. The specimen is visible at the bottom of the screen.

This patient had an uneventful postoperative course. On postoperative day one, she was passing flatus and tolerating liquids. Her diet was advanced and she was discharged to her home on post-operative day three. The patient returned to the office two weeks later with no complaints.
This video demonstrated a minimally invasive technique in the emergent setting. With proper experience, this is safe and leads to improved patient outcomes.
